title: machine: add mount command
body:
A new subcommand `mount` is added under `podman machine`. This command
runs [sshfs][1] to set up a mountpoint on local host but backed in a
directory from the VM.

This change is related to [#8016][2].

[1]: https://github.com/libfuse/sshfs
[2]: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8016

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *zhouhaibing089* To complete the [pull request process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process), please assign **mtrmac** after the PR has been reviewed. You can assign the PR to them by writing `/assign @mtrmac` in a comment when ready. The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
A brief scan and the changes look OK, but our CI system isn't happy .  You'll need to add a test or two.

@ashley-cui @baude PTAL.

I don't know about making this a separate, manually-invoked command - Podman should really be initiating the volume mounting itself. We also aren't firmly decided on using SSHFS before we investigate other options.

Wouldn't it make sense to mount a local folder on the remote rather than the opposite ? It would allow standard development on the host and running podman with files from host-local volumes. The best would even be to have the same absolute paths if possible. It would makes usage much closer to running podman fully locally.

SSHFS would then be needed on the VM only (I think it's there already) and is not needed on the host. The host needs sshd tough, with configured keys.

Something else I am wondering: how do we handle file ownership and UID differences ?

> Podman should really be initiating the volume mounting itself.

My take is, if inside the podman VM, we can see the same `/User/${USER}` as from the host, it should work for us - Running the mount command once doesn't seem to bother me at least.  I guess another idea is to move this mount as one step in `podman machine start`.

> We also aren't firmly decided on using SSHFS before we investigate other options.

Admittedly, `podman machine mount` seems to be too generic, if we want to be specific about `sshfs`, it probably should be `podman machine mount sshfs` and should provide the capability for other solutions.

> Wouldn't it make sense to mount a local folder on the remote rather than the opposite ?

Doing reverse sshfs mount seems to be tricky, but should be possible I guess. One problem with the default image is, we can't create new directory under `/` as it is read-only, so we are gonna need a different VM image.

@zhouhaibing089 

> Admittedly, podman machine mount seems to be too generic, if we want to be specific about sshfs, it probably should be podman machine mount sshfs and should provide the capability for other solutions.

The original name was `docker-machine sshfs`, but it was renamed to `docker-machine mount` to be more generic...  😉

https://docs.docker.com/machine/reference/mount/

In unrelated news, "rsync" was renamed to "scp --delta" because someone didn't want to add a new command for it.

https://docs.docker.com/machine/reference/scp/

@patjlm

> Wouldn't it make sense to mount a local folder on the remote rather than the opposite ? It would allow standard development on the host and running podman with files from host-local volumes. The best would even be to have the same absolute paths if possible. It would makes usage much closer to running podman fully locally.

Using sshfs was added as a simpler alternative, after the community failed to implement VirtualBox Shared Folders for the other drivers. There were some attempts, like using 9p (or even nfs), but most of them had awful performance compared to VM-local...

The community is still struggling with this, it seems. But there will be vsock and virtiofs support in QEMU "any day now".

https://github.com/dhiltgen/docker-machine-kvm/issues/2

There were also some security concerns with the default implementation in Docker, that would share your home folder...

https://xkcd.com/1200/

@zhouhaibing089 : I am not sure this command makes sense for the new Podman Machine, since the mounts are supposed to be transparent and go in the other direction for the new Podman Machine - similar to lima and similar to Docker Desktop ?

It made sense with docker-machine and podman-machine, since the VM was explicit there (and you could have more than one, and it could be remote in the cloud). But for the new Podman, maybe it would be more to add to the "mount list" in the VM.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
@zhouhaibing089 @afbjorklund Is this still being worked on?
I think it should probably be closed, in favor of having some _other_ solution ?

I wrote the sshfs mount command, and it works fine.

But I think that the new podman machine wants virtiofs (vsock) eventually, and virtfs (9p) until it is ready. Just needs it merged into qemu.
I think I'm just going to close it. :) Thanks for the huge discussion(and information) presented here and corresponding Github issue.
